industries.name: Semiconductors & Microelectronics
industries.share: ~40% of silicon use by value (though <5% by volume)
uses: Single-crystal silicon wafers grown by the Czochralski method — a seed crystal is pulled from molten hyperpure silicon (99.9999999%, 9N) to create ingots sliced into 200mm or 300mm wafers as the substrate for all CMOS chips
Polysilicon (polycrystalline silicon, 9N+ purity) is the feedstock for both semiconductor wafers and solar cells — produced by chemical vapor deposition (Siemens process) from trichlorosilane
Silicon-on-insulator (SOI) wafers for low-power and high-frequency chips, and silicon carbide (SiC) wafers for power electronics in electric vehicles and inverters
Silicon dioxide (SiO₂) gate dielectric, field oxide isolation, and interlayer dielectric in chip fabrication — silicon's native oxide is uniquely suited to MOSFET manufacturing
Doped silicon (boron for p-type, phosphorus/arsenic for n-type) forms the transistor junctions that are the basis of all digital logic, memory, and analog semiconductor devices

companies.name: Shin-Etsu Chemical
companies.use: World's largest silicon wafer producer. Manufactures 300mm and 200mm polished and epitaxial wafers supplied to TSMC, Samsung, Intel, SK Hynix, and Micron. Also largest polysilicon and silicone producer.

companies.name: SUMCO Corporation
companies.use: Second-largest silicon wafer manufacturer. Produces 300mm CZ and FZ wafers for logic and memory chips. Joint venture with Samsung for dedicated supply of advanced wafers.

companies.name: Siltronic (GlobalWafers)
companies.use: European silicon wafer producer acquired by GlobalWafers. Produces 300mm wafers for leading-edge logic and memory chip fabs including TSMC and GlobalFoundries.

companies.name: Wacker Chemie
companies.use: Major polysilicon producer using the Siemens CVD process. Supplies semiconductor-grade polysilicon to wafer manufacturers and solar-grade polysilicon to photovoltaic module producers.

companies.name: Hemlock Semiconductor
companies.use: Joint venture of DowCorning, Shin-Etsu, and Mitsubishi. Produces semiconductor and solar-grade polysilicon in Michigan. Major supplier to the US domestic chip and solar panel supply chain.

industries.name: Solar Photovoltaics
uses: Monocrystalline silicon solar cells (mono-Si) grown by Czochralski method achieve 22–24% efficiency — dominant technology in residential and commercial rooftop installations
Multicrystalline (polycrystalline) silicon solar cells cast from directionally solidified silicon blocks — lower cost but 18–20% efficiency; losing market share to mono-Si
Passivated emitter and rear cell (PERC) technology adds a reflective rear passivation layer to mono-Si cells, boosting efficiency and extending the viable life of silicon-based solar
TOPCon (tunnel oxide passivated contact) and heterojunction (HJT) cell architectures push mono-Si efficiency above 25% in mass production — becoming mainstream by 2025
Solar-grade polysilicon (6N purity, lower than semiconductor 9N) is the primary feedstock — approximately 5 grams of polysilicon produces 1 watt of solar capacity

companies.name: Tongwei Group
companies.use: World's largest solar-grade polysilicon producer. Operates massive low-cost polysilicon plants in Sichuan and Inner Mongolia powered by hydropower and coal. Also world's largest solar cell manufacturer.

companies.name: Daqo New Energy
companies.use: Major Chinese polysilicon producer in Xinjiang and Inner Mongolia. Among the world's lowest-cost polysilicon manufacturers due to integrated power generation and advanced Siemens reactor technology.

companies.name: LONGi Green Energy
companies.use: World's largest solar panel manufacturer and largest monocrystalline silicon wafer producer. Pioneered mass adoption of PERC and TOPCon high-efficiency cell technology in utility-scale solar.

companies.name: First Solar
companies.use: Largest US solar panel manufacturer and the only major non-silicon (cadmium telluride, CdTe) thin-film producer — but expanding into silicon-based manufacturing given supply chain pressures.

industries.name: Silicones & Specialty Chemicals
uses: Silicon metal reacts with methyl chloride at 250–300°C (Müller-Rochow direct process) to produce methylchlorosilanes — the precursors to all polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) silicone polymers
Silicone sealants and adhesives for construction, aerospace, automotive, and electronics — remain flexible from -60°C to +250°C, unaffected by UV and moisture
Silicone elastomers for medical implants (breast implants, joint replacements, catheters), baby bottle nipples, and food contact seals — biocompatibility and sterilizability are key properties
Silicone fluids as release agents, lubricants, and heat transfer fluids; silicone antifoams in food processing, fermentation, and paper manufacturing
Fumed silica (pyrogenic SiO₂) produced by flame hydrolysis of silicon tetrachloride — used as reinforcing filler in silicone rubber and as a thickener and anti-caking agent

companies.name: Dow Silicones (Dow Inc.)
companies.use: World's largest silicone producer. Converts silicon metal to silicone polymers, sealants, elastomers, and fluids at facilities in Michigan, Germany, and China for construction, healthcare, automotive, and electronics.

companies.name: Wacker Chemie
companies.use: Second-largest silicone producer. Produces PDMS polymers, silicone rubber, dispersions, and specialty silicones from its Burghausen site — the world's largest integrated silicone complex outside the US.

companies.name: Momentive Performance Materials
companies.use: Major silicone producer (formerly GE Advanced Materials). Produces silicone sealants, coatings, fluids, and elastomers for electronics encapsulation, personal care, and automotive applications.

companies.name: Shin-Etsu Chemical
companies.use: Japan's largest silicone producer and world's largest polysilicon producer. Supplies silicone rubber, fluids, and specialty silicones to electronics, automotive, and medical device manufacturers globally.

industries.name: Ferrosilicon & Metallurgy
industries.share: ~30% of silicon use by volume (ferrosilicon is ~70% Si)
uses: Ferrosilicon (FeSi, 45–90% Si) added to steel during electric arc furnace melting as a deoxidizer — silicon removes dissolved oxygen as SiO₂ slag, preventing gas porosity in castings
Silicon strengthens steel through solid solution hardening; silicon steel (electrical steel, 3–4.5% Si) has dramatically reduced magnetic core losses in transformers and electric motors
Ferrosilicon for production of silicon metal by further refining, and as alloying addition for cast irons, spring steels, and stainless steels
Silicon used in production of silicon carbide (SiC) abrasives for grinding wheels, sandpaper, and cutting tools, and SiC refractory materials for kiln furniture
Magnesium-ferrosilicon (MgFeSi) alloy used for production of ductile (nodular) cast iron — nodularizing treatment converts graphite flakes to spheroidal form

companies.name: Ferroglobe
companies.use: World's largest silicon metal and ferrosilicon producer outside China. Operates smelters in USA, Spain, France, South Africa, and Canada. Supplies silicon to aluminum, chemical, and semiconductor industries.

companies.name: Elkem
companies.use: Norwegian silicon and ferrosilicon producer with smelters powered by Norwegian hydroelectricity. Supplies silicon metal to silicone, aluminum, and solar industries; ferrosilicon to global steel producers.

companies.name: Dow (former DowCorning)
companies.use: Operates silicon metal production at Carrollton, Kentucky as feedstock for its silicone polymer operations — one of the few fully integrated US silicon metal-to-silicone producers.
